content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 323-3901 322-6441 322-6444

November 2, 1983

Robert L. Griffiths
National Unity Party
5335 College Oak Drive, #128
Sacramento, CA 95841
Re: FPPC No. A-83-248

Dear Mr. Griffiths:

This is to confirm our telephone conversation of
today, regarding the efforts of the National Unity Party
to qualify for the California State ballot.

You indicated that the Party, if it qualifies, will
run John Anderson for President in next year's election, and
asked whether the Party would incur any registration or
reporting obligations under California's Political Reform
Act based on this effort.

I advised you that this qualification effort is not
for "political purposes" as that term is used in the Political
Reform Act and, therefore, the Party is not required to
register as a committee and is not required to report its
financial activities.

If the Party were to receive contributions or make
expenditures in connection with supporting or opposing a
candidate for California state or local office, or a California
state or local ballot measure, the Party would become a
"committee" under California law and would incur reporting
obligations. You indicated that you are aware of this, and
would contact our office should the Party get involved in
such activity.

Thank you for your inquiry. Please contact me if
you have any questions.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Jeanne Pritchard
Political Reform Consultant

State of California

Dear Mr. Griffiths:

                                                    Re: FPPC NO. A-83-248

     This is to confirm our telephone conversation of
today, regarding the efforts of the National Unity Party
to qualify for the California State ballot.

     You indicated that the Party, if it qualifies, will
run John Anderson for President in next year's election, and
asked whether the Party would incur any registration or
reporting obligations under California's Political Reform
Act based on this effort.

     I advised you that this qualification effort is not
for "political purposes" as that term is used in the Political
Reform Act and, therefore, the Party is not required to
register as a committee and is not required to report its
financial activities.

     If the Party were to receive contributions or make
expenditures in connection with supporting or opposing a
candidate for California state or local office, or a California
state or local ballot measure, the Party would become a
"committee" under California law and would incur reporting
obligations. You indicated that you are aware of this, and
would contact our office should the Party get involved in
such activity.

     Thank you for your inquiry. Please contact me if
you have any questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Jeanne Pritchard
                                        Political Reform Consultant

sections.question: Would the National Unity Party incur any registration or reporting obligations under California's Political Reform Act based on efforts to qualify for the California State ballot to run John Anderson for President in the 1984 election?
sections.conclusion: This qualification effort is not for "political purposes" as that term is used in the Political Reform Act and, therefore, the Party is not required to register as a committee and is not required to report its financial activities. However, if the Party receives contributions or makes expenditures in connection with supporting or opposing a candidate for California state or local office, or a California state or local ballot measure, the Party would become a "committee" under California law and would incur reporting obligations.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a political party's efforts to qualify for the California State ballot as a vehicle to run a presidential candidate trigger registration and reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that ballot qualification efforts for a presidential campaign are not "political purposes" under the Political Reform Act and do not trigger registration or reporting requirements. However, any involvement in California state or local candidate or ballot measure activity would impose committee registration and financial reporting obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' headers but structure is present. Document confirms a prior telephone conversation. Minor OCR variations between two copies of same letter (e.g., 'Inferest' vs 'Interest').

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Would the National Unity Party incur any registration or reporting obligations under California's Political Reform Act based on efforts to qualify for the California State ballot to run John Anderson for President in the 1984 election?

CONCLUSION: This qualification effort is not for "political purposes" as that term is used in the Political Reform Act and, therefore, the Party is not required to register as a committee and is not required to report its financial activities. However, if the Party receives contributions or makes expenditures in connection with supporting or opposing a candidate for California state or local office, or a California state or local ballot measure, the Party would become a "committee" under California law and would incur reporting obligations.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advised that the National Unity Party's efforts to qualify for the California ballot to run John Anderson for President do not constitute "political purposes" under the Political Reform Act and thus do not require committee registration or financial reporting. The Party would only incur such obligations if it engages in activities supporting or opposing California state or local candidates or measures.
